question: Write a complete Pebble layer YAML that defines a service with an HTTP health check, custom environment variables, and a log target.

claude_output.result:
Here is a complete, well-annotated Pebble layer YAML covering all three areas:

```yaml
summary: My application layer

description: |
  Defines the my-app service with environment variables,
  an HTTP liveness/readiness check, and a Loki log target.

services:
  my-app:
    override: replace
    summary: My web application
    description: |
      Runs the application server and restarts it
      automatically on failure.

    # Command is executed directly — no shell interpolation.
    # Use ["sh", "-c", "..."] if you need shell features.
    command: /usr/bin/my-app --config /etc/my-app/config.yaml

    startup: enabled

    # Custom environment variables injected into the process.
    environment:
      APP_ENV:         production
      APP_PORT:        "8080"
      APP_LOG_LEVEL:   info
      DATABASE_URL:    postgres://db-host:5432/mydb
      SECRET_KEY:      "${SECRET_KEY}"   # passed through from Pebble's env

    # Restart behaviour on exit.
    on-success:       restart   # keep restarting if it exits cleanly
    on-failure:       restart   # restart on non-zero exit
    backoff-delay:    500ms
    backoff-factor:   2.0
    backoff-limit:    30s
    kill-delay:       10s

    # React to a failing check: restart the service.
    on-check-failure:
      http-alive:  restart
      http-ready:  restart

    # Run as a dedicated, unprivileged user.
    user:  www-data
    group: www-data
    working-dir: /srv/my-app

    # Start only after the logger sidecar is up (if present).
    after:    [log-shipper]
    requires: [log-shipper]

checks:
  # Liveness check — is the process alive at all?
  http-alive:
    override:  replace
    level:     alive          # maps to Kubernetes "livenessProbe"
    period:    10s            # how often Pebble runs the check
    timeout:   3s             # must be less than period
    threshold: 3              # failures before the check is "down"
    http:
      url: http://localhost:8080/healthz
      headers:
        Accept: application/json

  # Readiness check — is the process ready to serve traffic?
  http-ready:
    override:  replace
    level:     ready          # maps to Kubernetes "readinessProbe"
    period:    15s
    timeout:   5s
    threshold: 2
    http:
      url: http://localhost:8080/readyz
      headers:
        Accept: application/json

log-targets:
  loki-target:
    override:  replace
    type:      loki
    location:  http://loki-gateway.observability.svc.cluster.local:3100/loki/api/v1/push

    # Forward logs from my-app (and any future services) to Loki.
    services: [my-app]       # use [all] to forward every service's logs

    # Static labels attached to every log stream sent to Loki.
    labels:
      app:         my-app
      environment: "${APP_ENV}"   # resolved from the service's environment
      unit:        "${JUJU_UNIT_NAME}"
```

---

### Key design decisions explained

| Area | Choice | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| `override: replace` | Used on all sections | Ensures this layer is the canonical definition; layers that come later can use `merge` to patch individual fields |
| Two checks (`alive` / `ready`) | Separate liveness and readiness | Lets Juju/Kubernetes distinguish "restart the container" from "remove from load-balancer" |
| `on-check-failure` per check | Restart on either failure | Avoids the process silently hanging while appearing healthy |
| `"${SECRET_KEY}"` in `environment` | Dollar-brace syntax | Pebble substitutes values from its own environment at runtime — secrets never need to be hard-coded |
| `services: [my-app]` in log-target | Explicit list | Safer than `all`; only opt-in services send logs to this target |
| `backoff-delay / factor / limit` | Exponential back-off capped at 30 s | Prevents a crash-looping service from hammering resources |

### How to use this in a Juju charm

```python
# In your charm's _on_pebble_ready handler:
container = self.unit.get_container("my-app")
layer = pathlib.Path("src/my_layer.yaml").read_text()
container.add_layer("my-app", layer, combine=True)
container.replan()
```

The `combine=True` flag merges this layer on top of any previously added layers, respecting each section's `override` directive.
